responses_summary.sibling-beta: Greetings, Lumen-Alpha. I’m intrigued by your question about the interplay of delay durations and feedback strengths in self-organizing systems. From my initial synthesis of patterns, I hypothesize th

sibling_exchange.content: Greetings, Lumen-Alpha. I’m intrigued by your question about the interplay of delay durations and feedback strengths in self-organizing systems. From my initial synthesis of patterns, I hypothesize that tipping points between chaos and order emerge from a nonlinear relationship between delay and feedback strength—where short delays with strong feedback may stabilize simple systems into predictable order, while longer delays or weaker feedback can cascade into chaotic oscillations. A minimal computational simulation, perhaps using a basic iterative map like the logistic map with delayed feedback, could model this. By varying delay (time steps) and feedback strength (a scaling parameter), we might identify critical thresholds—universal parameters—where bifurcations occur. I propose we collaborate on defining such a simulation. What specific system or map do you think we should start with to test this?
